metadata.title: The computational analysis of digital traces in ethnographic studies of online communities
metadata.journal: Bulletin of Sociological Methodology/Bulletin de Méthodologie Sociologique
keywords: online communities Mots cl és analyse computationnelle

abstract: é L'analyse computationnelle des traces num ériques dans les études ethnographiques des communaut és en ligne. Cet article propose une approche méthodologique qui intègre l'analyse quantitative dans les études ethnographiques des communautés en ligne. En prenant en compte les traces des interactions dans les environnements numériques, il démontre les avantages potentiels de l'analyse computationnelle dans la conduite d'une ethnographie en ligne. L'application de méthodes quantitatives, peu associées a priori aux méthodes ethnographiques, enrichit l'observation des interactions qui se nouent dans les communautés en ligne. À travers une étude de cas d'une communauté sociologique en ligne de langue russe, l'article met en évidence les points de convergence entre les questions de recherche, favorisant un dialogue entre l'analyse computationnelle des traces numériques et l'ethnographie en ligne. Il décrit la mise en oeuvre de méthodes telles que l'analyse de contenu, l'analyse de réseau et la modélisation de sujets, tout en réfléchissant à leurs capacités analytiques dans le contexte de l'ethnographie en ligne.

sections.paper_id: Barkhatova - 2023 - The computational analysis of digital traces in ethnographic studies of online communities
paragraphs: In recent years, there has been a trend towards integrating computational analysis into ethnographic research (e.g., Berthod et al., 2017;Akemu and Abdelnour, 2018;Cottica et al., 2020;Campagnolo, 2022). A separate research area of "quantitative ethnography" has emerged that adapts statistical tools for ethnographic research (Shaffer, 2017;Kaliisa et al., 2021;Cai et al., 2021). The advantages of such methodological strategies include the opportunities to scale ethnography, to increase the transparency and replicability of results, and to reduce the issues of research validity (Abramson et al., 2018;Pajo, 2019). Additionally, quantitative methods, which are typically not associated with ethnography, can complement the qualitative analysis to achieve complex descriptions (Aipperspach et al., 2006).

In the realm of ethnographic research, the exploration of online communities has emerged as a significant area of study. These communities provide a wealth of data that can be conveniently analyzed using quantitative methods. Various aspects of online communication, including messages, comments, likes, hashtags, and more, are stored as digital traces, offering researchers the opportunity to delve into and analyze this rich archival content.

I present a methodological approach, developed in my doctoral research, that embraces the growing dialogue between computational analysis and ethnography in the study of interactions within online communities. By considering the computational analysis of digital traces as a comprehensive framework for ethnographic observation, this research aims to identify points of convergence and foster a meaningful dialogue between these two fields. To illustrate this approach, I delve into a case study of a Russian-speaking sociological online community. I address research questions that are pertinent to this integrated approach and explore the methods and techniques of computational analysis that can be effectively employed. Specifically, I discuss the incorporation of content analysis, network analysis, and topic modeling within the framework of ethnographic observation. Additionally, I provide a reflective analysis of their analytical capabilities within the context of online ethnography.

The article is organized into 5 sections. First, I analyze the approaches of online ethnography and show how its toolkit transforms from mixed mode to mixed methods. Then I discuss the existing designs of mixed methods when quantitative methods are integrated into ethnographic studies of online communities. Next, I describe the methodological foundations of the ethnographic approach, wherein content analysis, network analysis, and topic modeling are blended with ethnographic observation. After that, I outline the case of a Russian-speaking sociological online community, in which the proposed methodological approach was applied, and the ethical issues associated with the study. And the last section discusses the results, structured according to the tasks of the empirical case study.

sections.paper_id: Barkhatova - 2023 - The computational analysis of digital traces in ethnographic studies of online communities
sections.header_raw: From virtual to digital ethnography: from mixed mode to mixed methods
sections.header_norm: from virtual to digital ethnography from mixed mode to mixed methods
paragraphs: The methodological foundations of ethnography, developed long before the advent of the Internet, face challenges in applying them in the modern digital context (e.g., Markham, 2013;Abidin and de Seta, 2020;Winter and Lavis, 2020). The dynamic nature of communication in the online environment, devoid of clear spatial and temporal boundaries, necessitates the development of methodological solutions adapted to the study of online communities.

One of the first researchers who declared her experience in the field of ethnography on the Internet was Hine (2000), who introduced the approach of virtual ethnography. This approach is grounded on two theoretical views: the Internet as a culture and the Internet as a cultural artifact (Hine, 2000). The former means that the Internet is a space in which culture is created and reproduced. The latter shows that the Internet itself can be seen as a product of culture. Based on this theoretical background, ethnographic fieldwork is conceptualized as a mixture of online and offline practices (Airoldi, 2018), in which the research toolkit, as a rule, is based on combinations of online observations with offline interviews and/or offline observations (Garcia et al., 2009;Beneito-Montagut, 2011;Hallett and Barber, 2014). In terms of tools, virtual ethnography relies on adapting traditional ethnographic methods to the online context, virtualizing them (video interviews, chat interviews, email interviews, online observations, etc.) and combining with offline analogs (interviews, observations in offline settings).

In the transition from virtual ethnography to digital ethnography, the methodology places increasing emphasis on software and digital tools. In "netnography," as proposed by Kozinets (2010), researchers immerse themselves in community communication to reconstruct its culture and shared identity. Initially, this approach was developed to study online consumer communities (Kozinets, 2002), but subsequently it has spread beyond marketing, e.g. to the studies of travel forums, blogs, etc. While field immersion is often mediated by various data collection and analysis software (e.g., surveys or text encoding tools), at the epistemological level netnography aligns more closely with the paradigm of virtual methods. Caliandro (2018) proves this by the argument that the approach adapts traditional research procedures and ethnographic techniques to the online environment.

The complexity, variability, and fragmentation of the online environment both complicate the research process and create new methodological opportunities. Adopting a digital ethnography approach, which represents society as a network, allows for the analysis of how the digital setting shapes the flow of information and communication. This approach relies on digital methods that provide a fresh insight into measuring digital phenomena (Rogers, 2013). It provides a broader research focus than virtual ethnography. Digital ethnography enables researchers to capture the changes occurring within the digital environment. Presently, most online interactions occur within "liquid" metafields, such as news feeds or searches for specific keywords and hashtags on social media (Airoldi, 2018). Consequently, the data gathered consist of a massive and unorganized flow of comments, likes, and retweets. One outcome of such conceptualization has been the development of mixed methods that capture various dimensions in online ethnographic research, which I discuss further in the following section.

sections.paper_id: Barkhatova - 2023 - The computational analysis of digital traces in ethnographic studies of online communities
sections.header_raw: Mixed methods ethnographic research designs in digital settings
sections.header_norm: mixed methods ethnographic research designs in digital settings
paragraphs: The existing practices of mixing methods in digital ethnographies involve a variety of research designs. As with any other mixed methods research, mixed data analysis in online ethnography can be conducted in two forms: crossover analysis and integrative analysis 1 . Crossover analysis assumes quantitative methods to be implemented on qualitative data (the opposite direction is not characteristic for ethnography). In this case, qualitative data are analyzed predominantly using formal, mathematical or statistical methods (Small, 2011). Examples of such research designs include online ethnographic studies of Berhood et al. (2017), Cottica et al. (2020), and Akemu and Abdelnour (2018), in which interview and observation data were analyzed via quantitative methods (in the given examples, it was a network analysis). Other examples also include studies in the area of "quantitative ethnography" (e.g., Kaliisa et al., 2021), that use such methods as descriptive statistics, time series, inferential statistics, cluster analysis, Principal Component Analysis (PCA), social network analysis (SNA) and so on, on qualitative data.

Another form of mixed methods that can also be found in digital ethnographies, is integrative analysis. In this type of research, different analytical perspectives, on which qualitative and quantitative methods are grounded, yield a more comprehensive picture of phenomena studied than is possible from one perspective alone (Small, 2011). Following the purpose of methodological triangulation, Laaksonen et al. (2017) propose a "big-data-augmented ethnography" which considers computational analysis of digital traces as a technique to validate and generalize qualitative results. In their research of candidate interactions during an election campaign, the authors implement a parallel design, wherein ethnographic observations and computational analysis of digital traces are done at the same time.

It is not necessary to consider the integrative mixed methods as a process of triangulation on a singular research object. Many researchers prioritize the complementarity of methods rather than their triangulation. Given the complexity of the online environments, the use of quantitative methods can give a broader, more comprehensive picture of the phenomenon under study. Thus, "trace ethnography", proposed by Geiger and Ribes (2011), transforms observation notes and decodes documentary traces into thick descriptions. Such understanding of mixed methods found its implementation in online ethnographies with a sequential design, which assumes the sequential realization of quantitative and qualitative perspectives (or vice versa) and integrates them at the final stage of research.

In general, the stage of research at which quantitative methods are integrated into the overall design of the study can vary from initial to final. Howard (2002) proposes "network ethnography", wherein network analysis of technology-mediated interactions is seen as a starting point for further qualitative analysis. Hine (2015), on the contrary, started her research with participant observation and then she used the computational analysis of archival traces to place patterns observed into the broader context of the experience of the community studied.

A significant challenge for a researcher working with digital traces is that the categories of speech are technically mediated (through texts, voice messages, pictures, videos, etc.). Digital traces reflect interactions that occurred earlier, so they are archival content. The analysis of such data a priori lacks immersion. It resembles content analysis, which may cast some doubt on the ethnographic nature of the study (Gobo, 2008).

To summarize, quantitative methods can be considered as a complementary method of online ethnography. Disclosure of digital traces cannot be done without an ethnographic understanding of the activities, people and technologies which contribute to their production. However, at the same time, computational analysis of digital traces gives new methodological opportunities which today are not fully exploited. In my dissertation research, I propose an ethnographic approach that, due to a synergistic blend of quantitative methods with ethnographic observation, gives a more nuanced understanding of the structure and the interaction order in the online community. In the next section, I describe this approach through the prism of the empirical case study in which it was applied.

sections.paper_id: Barkhatova - 2023 - The computational analysis of digital traces in ethnographic studies of online communities
paragraphs: In this article, I introduce a mixed method ethnographic approach for studying interactions in online communities, which considers the computational analysis of digital traces as a stage of research which gives context for ethnographic observation. The methodological approach proposed was implemented in the empirical research of a Russianspeaking sociological online community on Facebook 2 . To comply with the ethical standards of anonymity, I do not mention the name of the online community.

Online ethnography of the professional sociological community was conducted for 6 years: from 2017 to 2022. The study was based on participant online observation, which was conducted covertly. Despite the asynchronous nature of communication in the online community under study, for greater contextualization, I tried to conduct the observation sessions predominantly in real time 3 .

In parallel, a computational analysis of digital traces was carried out to describe more comprehensively the community under study. As a basis for digital trace analysis, I used the content of the online community in 2017-2018 4 . It was obtained via two technologies for remote data collection from websites -API and scraping. Posts and comments were uploaded using the API connection. I used the 'Rfacebook' package 5 to get access to the platform's API via R (Barbera, 2017). The information on the composition of the participants and their socio-demographic characteristics was obtained via scraping realized in R packages -'rvest' (Wickham, 2019) and 'Rselenium' (Harrison and Kim, 2019).

The computational analysis of digital traces included the use of quantitative content analysis, social network analysis, and topic modeling. The focus of each method on solving its own research task made it possible to realize them in parallel.

Content analysis was used to describe the social composition of the participants. Due to the unstructured form of data on socio-demographic characteristics of participants, the recoding of variables was carried out manually in Excel. Given the research interest and the availability of socio-demographic variables for analysis, characteristics, such as gender, city of residence and place of work, were selected. Since the age of users is not disclosed by platform administration, it was not possible to estimate the age composition of the participants in the online community studied. Names and surnames in Russian are marked by gender and these were used to record the gender of the participants. For the city of residence and place of work, some data were missing due to empty profile sections. For some researchers, the data were restored using open sources: websites of universities, survey companies, and affiliation from the abstracts of recent articles.

The application of network analysis allowed me to visualize the network of communication activity and its homophily. To build a network of the online community studied, the data were transformed into an adjacency matrix, both rows and columns of which contain names of community members. I calculated some basic metrics of this network such as density, centralization, etc. As part of the second task, the socio-demographic characteristics were superimposed on the community network. After that, I analyzed to what extent their distribution of connections differs from random. The analysis of the network was conducted in R packages 'network' (Butts et al., 2019) and 'sna' (Butts, 2016). The visualization of the community structure was carried out in Gephi using a Force Atlas 2 layout.

Being a type of machine learning algorithm, this method can reduce the labor intensity and improve the quality of many research tasks that have traditionally been solved using content analysis (DiMaggio et al., 2013). The advantage of topic modeling is its ability to automatically reveal the thematic structure of communication, including determining which topic each of the analyzed texts belongs to. As an algorithm, one of the most well-known types of topic models was chosen: Latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) with Gibbs sampling. The analysis was conducted in the R software environment.

The construction of the model consisted of the following stages. First, the texts (namely, posts) were prepared for modeling. The corpus size was 574 posts. The decision not to include comments in the model was due to the fact that the posts in the online community were usually not short and rather detailed. In addition, I set the task of quantifying the popularity of the topics identified in the community. Accordingly, it was necessary to determine which of the topics each of the posts belongs to. I assumed that adding comments to the corpus could reduce the accuracy of matching posts and topics.

Using the 'text2vec' package (Selivanov et al., 2018), the texts were broken down into separate words and lemmatized, i.e. reduced to the dictionary form of the word. Then stop words were removed (prepositions, conjunctions, etc.), words that are ambiguous in Russian, as well as terms with a low frequency (I used the frequency < 4). The document-to-word frequency matrix is fed directly as LDA input via 'udpipe' package (Wijffels et al., 2019). The algorithm calculates two distributions: the distribution of words by topic and the distribution of texts (documents) by topics, selecting the necessary anti-aliasing parameters. As LDA is a probabilistic (generative) model, the resulting distributions are probabilistic (Blei, 2012). Each word appears on different topics with different probabilities, and the document itself is a mixture of several topics to which it belongs with different probabilities. Interpretation of topics is based on a list of the most frequently encountered words (top word list). They are presented in Table A in the Appendix. When choosing the optimal number of topics, I was guided by interpretation and analytical indicators illustrating the quality of topic models. Using the R package 'ldatuning' (Murzintcev and Chaney, 2019), a spectrum of the best solutions from a mathematical point of view was selected (see Figure A in the appendix). Focusing on a range from 12 to 22 topics, a solution with 16 topics chosen, as it gave the most reasonable interpretation.

To give a broader context and expand the interpretation by comparisons, I also conducted a content analysis of another channel for professional communication by sociologists -an academic conference (it was a small additional stage of research). I have chosen one of the largest sociological conferences in Russia -the International Grushinskaya Sociological Conference, in which both academic sociologists and practical sociologists take part. The analysis was carried out mainly on archival content -the video recordings of the sections and their text transcripts that are posted on the conference website.

In the following sections, I describe the realization of these methods in the study and give a reflection on their analytical capabilities in the context of online ethnography. However, first I give a more detailed description of the case and context.

sections.header_raw: The case and the ethical issues
sections.header_norm: the case and the ethical issues
paragraphs: The purpose of the empirical case study was to analyze and describe the structure, discussions, rules of interaction and conflicts in the online community. The starting point of the study was a problem of fragmentation which, according to Radaev (2013), is one of the key problems of the Russian sociological community. In this regard, it seemed important to pay attention to relatively new communication channel for Russian sociologists -professional online communities. For the study, I chose one of the largest and most well-known sociological online groups in Russia. It was created in 2011 by a famous Russian sociologist. At present, it has the format of a closed group (during the study it was changed several times, and there was even a conflict among the participants about this). At the time of the study, the total number of registered members was 2,612 users.

In the study, I designated this group as a community, because there is a rich emotional dynamic, and a collective identity is constructed in its discussions. As further analysis showed, the core of communication is built not so much around the pragmatic function of discussing professional issues, as around ritual interactions that generate collective symbols.

It is important to mention the ethical concerns. Traceability of data using search engines and access to sensitive and personal information expand traditional difficulties associated with obtaining informed consent and maintaining anonymity in ethnographic research (Walford, 2018). The choice of covert study of the Russian-speaking sociological online community is explained by the fact that the online community had been a public group for a long time and had a relatively loyal entry policy throughout the entire observation period. To anonymize the data, a "medium-cloaked" strategy 6 (Trevisan and Reilly, 2014) was adopted in the study. In the final report, I stated sensitive issues without disclosing personal information (names, geographical names and other identifying features of participants), and used direct quotations only if they could not be found using a search engine.

sections.paper_id: Barkhatova - 2023 - The computational analysis of digital traces in ethnographic studies of online communities
Barkhatova - 2023 - The computational analysis of digital traces in ethnographic studies of online communities
paragraphs: Based on information taken from the participants' profiles, the composition of community members was analyzed. The use of computational analysis made it possible to estimate a socio-demographic portrait of participants. Such an analysis of socio-demographic variables, usually conducted at the precoding stage of ethnographic research (before analyzing field notes) is used to facilitate the generalization to a larger and more representative sample or for comparative analytical purposes (LeCompte and Schensul, 2013). However, getting demographic variables from social media is complicated by hidden identities and pseudonyms on virtual sites (Sloan et al., 2013). In the analyzed case, it turned out that the overwhelming majority of profiles contain real names and surnames. According to my calculations, the share of pseudonyms does not exceed 3%. Many community members post photos on their profiles, indicating some basic information about themselves. Researchers do not conceal their identity in the online community and by the given names and pseudonyms they can be identified for further analysis.

It was revealed that the gender structure of the studied online community is characterized by a slight preponderance towards women (58%). The geography of the participants is represented mainly by Russian cities. Most of the participants live in Moscow (52%). Slightly more than a quarter of the participants live in other Russian cities (27%). Also, there is a small proportion of Russian-speaking researchers from the countries of the former USSR and European countries (9%). 13% of the participants did not indicate the city of their residence in the profile and it was impossible to identify it indirectly by other attributes (for example, place of work).

The community is dominated by representatives of academic science (48%) working in universities and academic institutions. 27% of the community members work in industry -mainly in survey centers, marketing firms or departments. 3% of the participants are representatives of professions far from sociology -doctors, artists, dancers, etc. who are not active in the community. It seemed impossible to determine the professional status of the remaining 22% of the participants. A significant part of them either simply did not fill out this information in their profiles, or it was impossible to identify their professional status from the information they provided.

Thus, the analysis of socio-demographic characteristics of participants gave a first glimpse of the online community. This information was used in further analysis of community structure, as well as useful for understanding of the topics which are discussed in the community.

sections.paper_id: Barkhatova - 2023 - The computational analysis of digital traces in ethnographic studies of online communities
paragraphs: To reveal the structure of the online community, I conducted network analysis. The use of network analysis allowed me to map the network of the community studied, as well as to access the homophily patterns. When choosing a criterion for building a network, I was guided by the fact that researchers may have known each other before interacting in the online community. Since there was no available information to determine how long ago the researchers became friends on Facebook, the analysis of "friendship networks" (e.g., Catanese et al., 2012) was left outside the scope of the study. In the research, I focused on analyzing their activities within the online community. The network was treated as weighted and directed. Incoming ties are getting comments on posts, and outgoing ties are writing comments on other participants' posts.

Figure 1 shows a network of communication activity within the online community. The structure of the network was described using the core-periphery model, which, as Xing et al. (2018) state, is rather typical for online forums and other groups of discussion. Thus, the community studied has a large proportion of peripheral participants (observers) who have not made any comments or posts (81%). Even without considering the passive majority, the density of the network, calculated as the ratio of the number of existing connections to the possible number of connections for a given network (Wasserman and Faust, 1994), is low (0.006). For this reason, further analysis was limited to active participants in the online community.

According to the various centralization metrics, which show a degree of rotation of the network around one node (actor) (Wasserman and Faust, 1994), the community is not centralized. Degree centralization, which shows the degree of direct influence of the most popular actor (his total number of direct ties) on communication within the community, is 0.258. Betweenness centralization is 0.150. It shows the degree of control of information flow in the network by one actor. The highest (0.327) in the given network is eigenvector centralization. It reflects the influence of the most significant actor in terms of social capital (number of links with the most "influential" actor) on the entire social network. Thus, it can be concluded that there is no clear leader in the community.

Further, individual structural positions of the participants were analyzed. In terms of prestige, which was measured as indegree (total number of incoming connections of an actor), there is a significant preponderance of participants without incoming ties (73% of the active participants). Such participants can be called "commentators": they give comments on other people's posts, but do not initiate threads themselves. Figure 2 shows the distribution of degree prestige for participants who have published at least one post in the community over an analyzed period. It can be seen that, on average, the participants got comments on their posts from about 11 participants.

Centrality, determined by the number of outgoing ties (outdegree), in the analyzed network reflects the number of people who were left comments by a particular member of the community. In contrast to prestige, most participants have non-zero centrality (only 7% of active participants did not leave comments on other people's posts). Figure 3 shows the distribution of the number of outgoing ties for participants. On average, the participants wrote comments to about 3 people, who wrote posts in the community over the analyzed period.

It is noteworthy that the same 10 people have the maximum number of incoming (prestige) and outgoing connections (centrality). Accordingly, these participants not only create the bulk of community content, but also receive an active response to their posts from other participants. Thus, these people can be considered as leaders of the online community.

Another task posed within network analysis was to assess the homophily in the community when various types of actors tend to associate with actors with the same characteristics. To check the homophily patterns, explained by place of work and city of residence, communicative activity was matched with the socio-demographic Figure 2. Distribution of the degree prestige (without zero indegree) Further, following the scheme of analysis of overall patterns of homophily proposed by Currarini et al. (2009), the percentage of links by place of work and city of residence were calculated. Table 1 shows the percentage distribution of connections (here without direction, which was considered during the analysis of the full network) depending on the place of work.

Among researchers working in academic sociology, who make up 50% of the active participants 7 , 29% of connections are with other colleagues from academic sociology and 56% with colleagues from industry. Accordingly, academic researchers are less likely to communicate with each other than if the connections were formed randomly (i.e., the percentage would be 50%). The percentage of connections between researchers from the industry who are active in the online community with each other in the constructed network coincides with a model of random connections. 30% of the connections among those working in industry are connections with each other, 53% are connections with those in academia.

It can be concluded that there are no homophily patterns, explained by the place of work in the analyzed community. A similar procedure was conducted for the city of residence 8 , for which no homophily patterns were detected (Barkhatova, 2020).

These results were intersected with the analysis of the sociological conference. Based on the information about the speakers posted on the conference website and found in the public domain, I manually coded their professional status (academic or industry specialist). Also, according to a similar scheme with the online community platform, the city of residence of the conference participants was coded. According to the degree of their participation in the discussions in the sections, their role was classified into speakers (those researchers who presented at least one report at the conference), observers (those who do not make a report and do not ask questions to the speakers) and commentators (those who ask questions to the speakers, but do not present a report at the conference). By this comparison I confirmed the conclusion that the specificity of professional online platforms clearly manifests in the online community studied. The online community has a higher proportion of peripheral participants (observers). On the community platform, academics and industry representatives more or less equally communicate with each other, while at the academic conference, due to thematic combination of reports in sessions, there is a division of sessions into academic and applied ones. In contrast to the Russian sociological community as a whole, which has a lack of sustainable dialogue between researchers from different regions and organizations (Sokolov and Titaev, 2013) and a high heterogeneity of sociologists in professional views (Zadorin, 2007), the fragmentation in the online community is not associated with place of work and city of residence.

Thus, the scheme of analysis implemented in the study can be used to check the density and influence of ties, as well as to access the homophily of online communities. For ethnography, which tends to view communities as homogeneous by default (Hine, 2000), homophily testing gives more insight into the structure of the community, which was useful for understanding its rules of interaction and conflicts. These sections are presented further in the text.

sections.paper_id: Barkhatova - 2023 - The computational analysis of digital traces in ethnographic studies of online communities
paragraphs: To identify the topics of discussion in the community, I used topic modeling. The topics obtained by the algorithm were divided into 4 thematic clusters: newsletter (advertising and announcements); public opinion surveys; methodological discussions; and research ethics, each containing several separate topics.

Newsletter (advertising and announcements). Most of the topics (n¼8) discussed by the community studied are various kinds of announcements and advertisements. First, these are announcements of various professional seminars. This topic consists mainly of words such as 'discussion', 'meeting', 'site', 'institute', 'seminar'. Announcements of conferences were divided by the algorithm into two independent topics: professional sociological conferences (words such as 'sociologist', 'section', 'report', 'conference', 'moderator' have the maximum probability of belonging to this topic) and marketing conferences ('marketing', 'client', 'speaker', 'program', etc.).

Another topic includes announcements of vacancies and the recruitment of employees in the company ('experience', 'vacancy', 'team', 'resume', 'offer', 'skill', etc.). There are also recruitment advertisements for various kinds of refresher courses ('school', 'qualification', 'skill', 'lecture', 'training', etc.), and announcements of major professional and educational projects ('motivational', 'participation', 'invite', 'registration', 'leader', 'specialist', etc.).

A significant part of information messages in the community is PR of books, articles and monographs that have just been published or are being published (this topic is dominated by words such as 'book ', 'discussion', 'interpretation', 'monograph', 'criticism', 'transformation'). Finally, as an independent topic, the algorithm also defined a professional meeting called 'Field Interviewers Form' ('field', 'forum', 'report', 'methodological', 'conversation', 'professional', 'methodologist', etc.), one of the organizers of which is the moderator of the online community.

The abundance of topics in the newsletter cluster indicates that the community is actively used by researchers to promote events and scientific products. In fact, this is a kind of professional advertising, the purpose of which is to attract the attention of the professional community.

Public opinion surveys. This cluster unites topics (n¼4) related to conducting sociological surveys. The topic of "organization of surveys" is more focused on discussing organizational issues: for example, how to conduct surveys in certain regions; how to maintain interactions with customers; how to find contractors. Such words as 'project', 'company', 'region', 'contractor', 'order' have the maximum probability of belonging to this topic.

The topic "quality of surveys" is more general, in comparison with the previous topic, which can be seen as at the level of vocabulary (this topic is dominated by words like 'monitoring ', 'opinion', 'collection', 'source', 'wording', 'material', 'joke', etc.), and at the level of content, combining the discussion of aspects of conducting sociological surveys, from methodology to results.

The two remaining topics are devoted to the discussion of sociological polls on political topics. The topic "socio-political survey methodology" reflects questions regarding the methodology of socio-political polls ('poll', 'sample', 'elections', 'telephone', 'distribution', 'formulation', etc.). Another topic is related to the discussion of socio-political survey results (such words as 'Russia ', 'attitude', 'vote', 'politics', 'majority', 'support', 'population', etc.).

Methodological discussions. The other three topics involve discussions devoted to methodological issues. The most common of them is the topic "general methodological issues", which brings together a very wide range of methodological discussions: from the selection of literature to the interpretation of results and writing analytical reports. In fact, this topic is mainly methodological advice, as evidenced by the abundance of requests in the list of top words ('resource', 'recommend', 'ask', 'data', 'analysis', 'problem', etc.).

The topic "student projects" is more focused on projects for young colleagues. Within this topic, the results of field expeditions are discussed in the context of methodology, and more experienced sociologists advise novice researchers on the choice of methods for collecting and analyzing data. This topic is very broad, since it combines a discussion of qualitative and quantitative methods ('student', 'expedition', 'prompt', 'advantage', 'result', 'coefficient', 'chi-square', 'residuals', etc.).

As an independent topic, the algorithm revealed a topic dedicated to purely qualitative methodology, which is lexically traced at the level of the words from which it is formed ('question', 'interviewer', 'interview', 'field', 'guide', 'expert', etc.).

Research ethics. The cluster "research ethics" is represented by only one topic -"publication ethics", as indicated by the dominance of such words as 'journal', 'council', 'ethics', 'editor', 'academician', 'publisher', 'article', 'editorial', 'anti-plagiarism', 'norm'. Accordingly, other aspects of research ethics (for example, ethics of relations with informants) are not discussed.

Next posts were categorized by topic, which allowed me to quantify the popularity of the topics identified in the community. As LDA treats each document (here posts) as a mixture of several topics, for the unambiguous assignment of texts to topics, the belonging of the document to the topic was determined according to the maximum probability. Data on the popularity of posts in the community contained three indicators: 1) topic size, which was defined as the total number of posts within the topic, 2) the average number of likes that post on a certain topic gets, 3) the average number of likes that post on a certain topic gets. Figure 5 illustrates the popularity of the topics identified.

The analysis conducted showed that the most numerous are survey topics. A significant part of it is made up of socio-political research. Despite the prevalence of survey topics in the community, their popularity is average. The topics of greatest interest include qualitative methods, publication ethics and the professional seminar "Field Interviewers Forum", which was actively promoted by the community moderator. Other topics from the cluster "Newsletter (advertising and announcements)" generate relatively few responses.

Thus, the implementation of LDA topic modeling helped me to identify topics for discussion in the community and to assess their popularity. The method used has shown its effectiveness on condition of understanding professional sociological vocabulary and slang, as well as a thorough preprocessing of data. As topic modeling gives a very rough estimate of word co-occurrences resulting, it was also backed up with qualitative validation of topics.

For each post, a topic was manually defined from a list, after which all posts were checked for compliance with the assigned topic. It turned out that for more than 80% of posts, the topic automatically assigned by the algorithm coincided with the results of manual coding. In other cases, the differences were mainly due to inaccuracies in assigning the probabilities of belonging to the topic. Another topic was more clearly manifested in the text with a sufficiently high, but not with maximum probability. This was most often observed in the case of broad vague topics (such as organization of surveys, quality of surveys, general methodological issues, student projects) rather than smaller and more specific ones. Speaking about the quality of the topics, in general, according to my observations, the topics identified quite fully reflect the topics of discussion in the community. The 16 topics found by the algorithm were easily grouped by meaning into 4 thematic clusters even at the stage of interpreting the topic model. During the subsequent verification of the correspondence of the posts to the topic assigned to them, the posts were reread once again, which made it possible to make sure that the topic model turned out to be quite meaningful.

As with social structure, the online community was compared with another channel for professional communication by sociologists -the sociological conference. The task of this analysis was to check whether the topics identified in the online community are typical for the professional communication of sociologists and whether they appear at the conference. With the help of manual deductive coding of the conference topics, the presented scheme with 4 thematic clusters was superimposed on the topics of the sections identified on the basis of the titles and abstracts of the sections and conference reports. Some thematic features of the discussions were found. In the online community, a significant part of the messages is a newsletter with announcements of upcoming events, the release of books, and so on. Compared to the conference format, more attention is paid to the discussion of methodological and ethical issues with less focus on other subject areas of sociology. The discussions are less formal, but more conflicting (this aspect is discussed in the next section).

Despite the abundance of topics related to announcements and practical issues (where to look for a contractor, what method of analysis to use, etc.), the further qualitative analysis showed the core of interaction is not built around the pragmatic function, as may seem from the results of topic modeling. With a deeper immersion in the communication, it turns out that solidarity in the community occurs through ritual interactions that generate collective symbols. This process can be seen in discussions about various situations when the media publish articles that incorrectly interpret the results of sociological surveys. Unlike posts of a pragmatic nature, there is a rich emotional dynamic here. The collective identity of a professional sociologist, who opposes a journalist with his "shifted" understanding of sociology, is being constructed.

Another example is the discussion of the timing and quality of peer review in scientific journals. Due to the multiplicity of roles, various, sometimes contradictory collective symbols are constructed. In the situation described, the researcher submitted an article to a Russian journal, but according to him, he waited a long time for a review and received, in his opinion, unreasonable comments. With his post, he tried to construct the image of an "incompetent" reviewer, who, moreover, delayed the review. His arguments were supported by some participants. However, many researchers wrote in the comments that they themselves periodically act as reviewers and in this regard they understand this work well from the inside. And in parallel, another set of collective symbols was constructed -"responsible" and "difficult unpaid additional work".

From a methodological point of view, the combination of quantitative and qualitative methods in this case made it possible to interpret the selected topics more comprehensively. Without a qualitative stage, a more pragmatic perception of discussions in the community was developed, i.e. the emotional aspect was lost. The use of quantitative methods has made it possible to assess the popularity of various topics in the community. It seemed that if the community had a lot of posts on socio-political surveys, they might be interesting for the participants, but quantitative analysis showed no high response. As a result, it was concluded that the community members were probably tired of these discussions.

The topic "student projects" is more focused on projects for young colleagues. Within this topic, the results of field expeditions are discussed in the context of methodology, and more experienced sociologists advise novice researchers on the choice of methods for collecting and analyzing data. This topic is very broad, since it combines a discussion of qualitative and quantitative methods ('student', 'expedition', 'prompt', 'advantage', 'result', 'coefficient', 'chi-square', 'residuals', etc.).

As an independent topic, the algorithm revealed a topic dedicated to purely qualitative methodology, which is lexically traced at the level of the words from which it is formed ('question', 'interviewer', 'interview', 'field', 'guide', 'expert', etc.).

Research ethics. The cluster "research ethics" is represented by only one topic -"publication ethics", as indicated by the dominance of such words as 'journal', 'council', 'ethics', 'editor', 'academician', 'publisher', 'article', 'editorial', 'anti-plagiarism', 'norm'. Accordingly, other aspects of research ethics (for example, ethics of relations with informants) are not discussed.

Next posts were categorized by topic, which allowed me to quantify the popularity of the topics identified in the community. As LDA treats each document (here posts) as a mixture of several topics, for the unambiguous assignment of texts to topics, the belonging of the document to the topic was determined according to the maximum probability. Data on the popularity of posts in the community contained three indicators: 1) topic size, which was defined as the total number of posts within the topic, 2) the average number of likes that post on a certain topic gets, 3) the average number of likes that post on a certain topic gets. Figure 5 illustrates the popularity of the topics identified.

The analysis conducted showed that the most numerous are survey topics. A significant part of it is made up of socio-political research. Despite the prevalence of survey topics in the community, their popularity is average. The topics of greatest interest include qualitative methods, publication ethics and the professional seminar "Field Interviewers Forum", which was actively promoted by the community moderator. Other topics from the cluster "Newsletter (advertising and announcements)" generate relatively few responses.

Thus, the implementation of LDA topic modeling helped me to identify topics for discussion in the community and to assess their popularity. The method used has shown its effectiveness on condition of understanding professional sociological vocabulary and slang, as well as a thorough preprocessing of data. As topic modeling gives a very rough estimate of word co-occurrences resulting, it was also backed up with qualitative validation of topics.

For each post, a topic was manually defined from a list, after which all posts were checked for compliance with the assigned topic. It turned out that for more than 80% of posts, the topic automatically assigned by the algorithm coincided with the results of manual coding. In other cases, the differences were mainly due to inaccuracies in assigning the probabilities of belonging to the topic. Another topic was more clearly manifested in the text with a sufficiently high, but not with maximum probability. This was most often observed in the case of broad vague topics (such as organization of surveys, quality of surveys, general methodological issues, student projects) rather than smaller and more specific ones. Speaking about the quality of the topics, in general, according to my observations, the topics identified quite fully reflect the topics of discussion in the community. The 16 topics found by the algorithm were easily grouped by meaning into 4 thematic clusters even at the stage of interpreting the topic model. During the subsequent verification of the correspondence of the posts to the topic assigned to them, the posts were reread once again, which made it possible to make sure that the topic model turned out to be quite meaningful.

As with social structure, the online community was compared with another channel for professional communication by sociologists -the sociological conference. The task of this analysis was to check whether the topics identified in the online community are typical for the professional communication of sociologists and whether they appear at the conference. With the help of manual deductive coding of the conference topics, the presented scheme with 4 thematic clusters was superimposed on the topics of the sections identified on the basis of the titles and abstracts of the sections and conference reports. Some thematic features of the discussions were found. In the online community, a significant part of the messages is a newsletter with announcements of upcoming events, the release of books, and so on. Compared to the conference format, more attention is paid to the discussion of methodological and ethical issues with less focus on other subject areas of sociology. The discussions are less formal, but more conflicting (this aspect is discussed in the next section).

Despite the abundance of topics related to announcements and practical issues (where to look for a contractor, what method of analysis to use, etc.), the further qualitative analysis showed the core of interaction is not built around the pragmatic function, as may seem from the results of topic modeling. With a deeper immersion in the communication, it turns out that solidarity in the community occurs through ritual interactions that generate collective symbols. This process can be seen in discussions about various situations when the media publish articles that incorrectly interpret the results of sociological surveys. Unlike posts of a pragmatic nature, there is a rich emotional dynamic here. The collective identity of a professional sociologist, who opposes a journalist with his "shifted" understanding of sociology, is being constructed.

Another example is the discussion of the timing and quality of peer review in scientific journals. Due to the multiplicity of roles, various, sometimes contradictory collective symbols are constructed. In the situation described, the researcher submitted an article to a Russian journal, but according to him, he waited a long time for a review and received, in his opinion, unreasonable comments. With his post, he tried to construct the image of an "incompetent" reviewer, who, moreover, delayed the review. His arguments were supported by some participants. However, many researchers wrote in the comments that they themselves periodically act as reviewers and in this regard they understand this work well from the inside. And in parallel, another set of collective symbols was constructed -"responsible" and "difficult unpaid additional work".

From a methodological point of view, the combination of quantitative and qualitative methods in this case made it possible to interpret the selected topics more comprehensively. Without a qualitative stage, a more pragmatic perception of discussions in the community was developed, i.e. the emotional aspect was lost. The use of quantitative methods has made it possible to assess the popularity of various topics in the community. It seemed that if the community had a lot of posts on socio-political surveys, they might be interesting for the participants, but quantitative analysis showed no high response. As a result, it was concluded that the community members were probably tired of these discussions.

sections.paper_id: Barkhatova - 2023 - The computational analysis of digital traces in ethnographic studies of online communities
sections.header_raw: Revealing the rules of interaction and deconstructing the conflicts
sections.header_norm: revealing the rules of interaction and deconstructing the conflicts
paragraphs: Another task of the empirical case study was to reveal the rules of interaction and conflicts in the online community. Unlike the previous sections, which were built primarily on the results of quantitative analysis, the description of the rules of interaction and conflicts was based mostly on qualitative data.

At the time of the study, there was only one explicit rule in the community, called "Ban on obliteration". According to this rule, the moderator and the participants cannot delete posts or comments if they start a discussion with 10 or more comments. This was justified by freedom of expression, and a minimum limit of 10 comments was chosen to allow the moderator to delete fake posts and comments. The existence of such a rule can be explained by the fact that the community seeks to reduce the interference that inevitably occurs in online communication associated with trolling, propaganda and unverified information.

Another rule of interaction that is advanced by the moderator, but is not fixed in written form, is the problematization of discussions. Instead of announcements of books, conferences, and other professional activities, the moderator encourages participants to create content which problematizes current professional issues. The presence of such an attitude from the moderator contributes to the development of the emotional dynamics of discussions. Communication is filled with collective meanings through which the professional identity of sociologists is formed.

In case of violation of these rules by the participants, the moderator issues a warning to them. As usual, no one, except the moderator, reacts to violators of order in the community. At the same time, in the case of warnings about the newsletter, he makes them selectively. According to my observations, he makes fewer comments to more experienced and well-known researchers in a professional environment. He sometimes uses the online platform to draw the attention of the professional community to his own projects and publications.

In order to maintain the interest in the online platform, the moderator occasionally initiated small entertaining voting on controversial questions from field experience. Also, he posted some jokes and films on professional topics, and encouraged other participants to join and supplement the collection. Such discussions, although they do not appear very often, also contribute to the development of emotional dynamics in the online community and the crystallization of professional identity.

Despite the moderator's active role in managing the community, his influence on other members is limited. During the analyzed period, there was a conflict about the moderation policy in the online community. Without going into a detailed description of the chronology of this conflict (see Barkhatova, 2020), I can mark its beginning by the unauthorized removal of the post by one of the community moderators (after this conflict, he was excluded from the moderators). Also, without discussing with other members, he changed the status of the group from closed to open. Many of the participants openly expressed dissatisfaction with the activities of the moderator. As a result, a vote was initiated to deprive the moderator of his powers. By majority decision, he was excluded from the moderators. Accordingly, it can be concluded that the community can self-organize at certain moments, and that collective decisions and actions can also regulate the moderation policy.

Many researchers have advocated closing the online community back. From their point of view, the professional community is inherently a closed group, access to which must be carefully regulated by a moderator. As an ideal model for the professional online community, the participants proposed the format of a closed English club, which can only be entered by invitation. Thus, it can be concluded that the community has a request for the professionalization of discussions, as the presence of non-professionals in the community blurs professional identity and interferes with professional communication. A relatively small part of the participants opposed the closure of the online community, expressing the opinion that the public has the right to observe and participate in professional discussions. This position is close to public sociology, which tries to take professional discussions beyond the academic framework and involve a wide audience in them. The consequence of a difference in opinions was the closure of the borders of the community, which for a long time remained in the format of an open group, but then was returned to the original closed format.

From a methodological point of view, the obtained results are consistent with the conclusion made on the basis of network analysis about the centralization of the community. There is no clear leader in the community, but there is a small core group of the most active participants who set the agenda for discussions. Due to his status, the moderator supports the enforcement of the rules in the community. However, his own activities are also regulated by the members of the community. As one of the participants wrote, the community should not be allowed to become a "private group".

In continuation of the analysis of conflicts in the community, it is worth noting discussion and development of already existing contradictory situations that occurred earlier in the offline environment. The most striking example of such a conflict are discussions about political bias in the results of surveys. In accordance with the way these discussions are built in the community, it makes sense to consider them through the confrontation between two large Russian centers for the study of public opinion. In the professional environment, this conflict has long historical roots and was associated with a change in leadership in one of these polling centers. Following the departure of the former leader, the rest of the staff moved with him to the new company. Some sociologists call this situation a "raider takeover". As was revealed during the observation, such a definition is traced in the communication of community members. When discussing surveys conducted by this public opinion research center, some members of the online community may not always remain within the bounds of objective professional criticism. Numerous posts and comments show that some participants get personal in discussions and even issue their own proposals as evidence of violations of professional standards. Such discussions often arise almost out of the blue, because of the fact that the participants tend to move away from professional discussions, remembering old grievances against each other. Putting the results in a broader context, I can conclude that there is an ideological conflict in the online community, which is consistent with the studies of the Russian sociological community of Zdravomyslova (2008), Safonova (2012), andSokolov (2019).

The presence of an ideological conflict in the community can significantly supplement the conclusions obtained using network analysis. The absence of patterns of homophily in the place of work and city of residence, with a weak density of ties within the community, probably means that fragmentation in the community can be caused to a greater extent by ideological differences of views and conflicts associated with this. However, the key finding of the study is that despite conflicts and contradictions, the online community performs an integrating function, facilitating communication between researchers from different cities, universities, and areas of sociology (Barkhatova, 2020).

There is no clear leader in the community, but there is a small core group of the most active participants who set the agenda for discussions. Due to his status, the moderator supports the enforcement of the rules in the community. However, his own activities are also regulated by the members of the community. As one of the participants wrote, the community should not be allowed to become a "private group".

In continuation of the analysis of conflicts in the community, it is worth noting discussion and development of already existing contradictory situations that occurred earlier in the offline environment. The most striking example of such a conflict are discussions about political bias in the results of surveys. In accordance with the way these discussions are built in the community, it makes sense to consider them through the confrontation between two large Russian centers for the study of public opinion. In the professional environment, this conflict has long historical roots and was associated with a change in leadership in one of these polling centers. Following the departure of the former leader, the rest of the staff moved with him to the new company. Some sociologists call this situation a "raider takeover". As was revealed during the observation, such a definition is traced in the communication of community members. When discussing surveys conducted by this public opinion research center, some members of the online community may not always remain within the bounds of objective professional criticism. Numerous posts and comments show that some participants get personal in discussions and even issue their own proposals as evidence of violations of professional standards. Such discussions often arise almost out of the blue, because of the fact that the participants tend to move away from professional discussions, remembering old grievances against each other. Putting the results in a broader context, I can conclude that there is an ideological conflict in the online community, which is consistent with the studies of the Russian sociological community of Zdravomyslova (2008), Safonova (2012), andSokolov (2019).

The presence of an ideological conflict in the community can significantly supplement the conclusions obtained using network analysis. The absence of patterns of homophily in the place of work and city of residence, with a weak density of ties within the community, probably means that fragmentation in the community can be caused to a greater extent by ideological differences of views and conflicts associated with this. However, the key finding of the study is that despite conflicts and contradictions, the online community performs an integrating function, facilitating communication between researchers from different cities, universities, and areas of sociology (Barkhatova, 2020).

sections.paper_id: Barkhatova - 2023 - The computational analysis of digital traces in ethnographic studies of online communities
paragraphs: In this article, I explore the possibilities offered by computational analysis of digital traces in the realm of online ethnographic research. Through the case study of a Russianspeaking sociological online community, I propose a synergistic blend of quantitative methods that effectively complement ethnographic observation, resulting in a more nuanced understanding.

By dividing the computational analysis methods (content analysis, network analysis, and topic modeling) into distinct tasks, any potential contradictions between them are mitigated, enabling their simultaneous implementation. Moreover, the integration of quantitative analysis with ethnography does not compromise the fundamental principles of ethnographic research. Key tenets such as attending to the local context, embracing the perspectives of community members, researcher immersion, reflexivity, thick description, and consideration of power dynamics (Hammersley and Atkinson, 1995;Angrosino, 2008;Fetterman, 2009;LeCompte and Schensul, 2010) remain integral to the process.

Echoing the methodological ethos of ethnography, which revolves around "telling social stories", Murthy (2008) concludes that the epistemological scope of ethnography remains unchanged even in the digital age. Similarly, the integration of computational analysis techniques into online ethnography brings about a transformation in storytelling methods without fundamentally contradicting the principles at its core.

The research questions, data collection techniques, and quantitative analysis methods employed in this study are adaptable and can be applied to analyze interactions within various types of online communities, extending beyond professional spheres. It is worth noting, however, that a researcher's familiarity with the community under investigation is crucial for the successful implementation of the proposed methodological approach. In cases where pre-immersion is lacking, incorporating interviews with community participants would serve as a valuable supplement.

The methodological contribution of this study lies in presenting novel opportunities for dialogue and convergence between different datasets. For instance, analyzing the community's structural centralization sheds light on moderation policies and the role of moderators in community management. Exploring patterns of homophily proves valuable in understanding conflicts within the community, as exemplified in this study by highlighting the significant influence of ideological differences among participants.

Another intersection of data is observed in the analysis of discussion topics. Solely relying on topic modeling without incorporating observational findings could give rise to a distorted perception of a predominantly pragmatically driven community. However, it becomes evident that a vibrant emotional dynamic permeates the community, with ritual interactions generating collective symbols. Consequently, the proposed combination of quantitative methods may not fully capture the emotional nuances of participant interactions. Conversely, relying solely on ethnographic observations would make it challenging to accurately describe the community's structure and gauge the popularity of different topics among community members.
